body_txt: In fighting manga / anime , superhero comics, and kung fu movies, characters are often stronger and tougher than any normal person has the right to be. This makes for more spectacular and compelling action scenes because it automatically limits the number of people/characters who can resolve a given conflict, thus pulling our favorite overpowered heroes into the fray. However, when this concept is taken to its logical extreme, a new problem arises: You have no idea who is winning or losing. When two supercharged beings hit each other with enough force to shatter a brick wall, yet they brush it off anyway because they are Nigh Invulnerable , we—as viewers—have no means of determining whether any such blows are significant or not. The character may reel, but after a moment he's back in the fight as if it had never happened. This is easy to accept at first, but if the fight goes on too long, you start to get lost and no longer feel the dramatic punch of each individual blow. Additionally, unless the writer finds an elegant way to make it feel clear and decisive, the conclusion will feel somewhat anti-climactic, since the whole fight was basically just an exchange of blows until one of them arbitrarily collapses from taking a blow which doesn't look any different from all the others they received . You know this problem is occurring when you, J. Random Viewer, have no idea which moves are supposed to be the deadly finishers and which ones are just throwaway moves and desperation attacks; or who has the advantage and who's on the ropes. It's almost like the combatants are fighting not to win, but to entertain and impress some invisible audience of spectators. But that would be silly, right? In short: this problem occurs when you, as a viewer, are not so much rooting for one of the characters to defeat the other as you are instead wondering when the writer will decide that the fight has "gone on long enough" and finally just have one of the characters die already. Obviously, the breaking point on this is a bit subjective. However, it can be especially damaging to a viewer's Willing Suspension of Disbelief when the Sorting Algorithm of Evil is in play: you never get to personally witness the assured growth in potency of either the heroes or the villains. The visual interpretation of combat in most Video Games follows this trope to a T. In many, characters literally show no signs of weakening (with perhaps the exception of a weakened pose when they drop to a certain number of Hit Points ) but this rarely causes any decrease in power. Until you suffer a Critical Existence Failure , you're just as effective (maybe even more ) as you ever were. This is usually compensated for by the fact that video games usually have visible health bars so that players can gauge the amount of damage an enemy is taking and how many more hits the player character can take. It is also an acceptable because it allows for a lot of combat in games without requiring the player character(s) to mend their wounds for a week after each fight, lest they become increasingly ineffective against a large supply of monsters that are only ever encountered with full hit points. When one side is getting all the damage and you still are wondering (slightly nauseated) how much more he can take, the trope is No-Holds-Barred Beatdown . See No Permanence, No Stakes for when this problem causes an audience to check out of the action entirely and thus lose interest in a story. If the audience can't tell who is winning or losing, then there is no reliable way of assessing the stakes . And if the audience can't tell just how serious the conflict and stakes are, how can they be expected to care about and root for anything? See also My Kung-Fu Is Stronger Than Yours , Only a Flesh Wound , and Rasputinian Death . Contrast Combat Breakdown , which often happens as both fighters tire out or wear down.
